01_initial_request.target_name: Edge-Cloud data anomaly detection framework based on iterative optimization
01_initial_request.input_data: Data collected by underlying sensor networks in IoT applications
01_initial_request.user_intent: To address the failure of traditional outlier detection methods in terms of latency and accuracy affecting IoT system decision-making
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Improve the accuracy of anomaly detection while maintaining low latency

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] IoT sensors generate large amounts of data that need efficient collection and processing. [Gap] Direct transmission of all data consumes high bandwidth and energy, and nodes need organization. [Decision] To achieve efficient data collection, chose to use Adaptive k-means algorithm to perform clustering on sensor nodes, expecting to obtain organized clusters for data aggregation.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Sensor nodes are aggregated into clusters, reducing communication cost and response latency.

references.citation: Bhatia SK (2004) Adaptive k-means clustering. In: FLAIRS conference, pp 695–699 [36]
references.relevance: Provides the clustering algorithm used to organize sensor nodes into clusters.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Anomaly detection model requires labeled training data to distinguish normal from abnormal samples. [Gap] Ground truth labels are not initially available for the unsupervised sensor data. [Decision] To achieve initial data labeling, chose to use Principal Component Score Difference (PCA-TS) to perform anomaly detection on clustered data, expecting to obtain training data D with initial normal/abnormal labels.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Generated training data D based on passed data where f(x)=1, identifying initial anomalies.

references.citation: Abdi H, Williams LJ (2010) Principal component analysis. Wiley Interdiscip Rev Comput Stat 2(4):433–459 [39]
references.relevance: Provides the mathematical foundation for PCA used in the score difference method.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] A classification model is needed to detect anomalies based on the labeled training data. [Gap] Traditional single-kernel models may lack robustness for multi-source IoT data. [Decision] To achieve robust classification, chose to use Multi-Kernel Support Vector Data Description (MKSVDD) to perform model construction on training data D, expecting to obtain decision function g(x).
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Constructed initial detection model with decision function g(x), achieving initial accuracy of 92.79%.

references.citation: Tax DM, Duin RP (2004) Support vector data description. Mach Learn 54(1):45–66 [37]
references.relevance: Provides the core SVDD method extended to Multi-Kernel SVDD.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] MKSVDD performance depends heavily on hyperparameters like penalty factor and kernel coefficients. [Gap] Optimal parameter values are unknown and manual tuning is inefficient. [Decision] To achieve optimal model configuration, chose to use Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm to perform parameter optimization on MKSVDD, expecting to obtain optimal penalty factor and kernel parameters.

parameters.optimization_targets: Penalty factor, multicore kernel function coefficients, MKSVDD kernel parameters

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Obtained optimal parameters used for further data cleaning and model initialization.

references.citation: Kennedy J, Eberhart R (1995) Particle swarm optimization. In: Proceedings of ICNN'95 [38]
references.relevance: Provides the optimization algorithm used to tune MKSVDD parameters.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Initial model accuracy (92.79%) needs improvement to meet decision-making requirements. [Gap] Static model cannot adapt to dynamic data streams or resolve detection conflicts. [Decision] To achieve improved accuracy, chose to use Online Machine Learning (Stochastic Gradient Descent) to perform iterative optimization on the model, expecting to obtain updated decision function G(x) with higher accuracy.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Model accuracy improved to 96.70% after iterative optimization (approx. 5 iterations for 500 data).

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The proposed framework claims to improve accuracy and maintain low latency. [Gap] Empirical evidence on real datasets is required to validate these claims. [Decision] To achieve validation, chose to use PyCharm Community Edition to perform simulation experiments on IBRL dataset, expecting to obtain quantitative metrics (Accuracy, TPR, FPR, Latency).
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Experiments showed accuracy of 96.70%, improved TPR/FPR, and low latency compared to TCM, ABODM, MDCM.

references.citation: Madden S (2004) Intel lab data. Web page, Intel [40]
references.relevance: Provides the real-world dataset used for experimental validation.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Simulation on real dataset (IBRL) with comparative analysis against baseline methods (TCM, ABODM, MDCM, SVM, SVDD)

accuracy.interpretation: Final anomaly detection accuracy after iterative optimization, improved from 92.79%.

accuracy_improvement.interpretation: Increase in accuracy achieved through the iterative optimization process.

true_positive_rate.interpretation: TPR changed smoothly with anomaly rate, showing higher values than baseline models.

false_positive_rate.interpretation: FPR remained low compared to other methods, indicating fewer false alarms.

F1_score.interpretation: IMKSVDD maintained leading position in AUC and competitive F1, balancing TPR and accuracy.

AUC.interpretation: IMKSVDD had the best AUC value among compared algorithms (SVM, SVDD, MKSVDD).

latency.interpretation: CPCA model maintained low latency compared to TCM (which exploded with dimension) and others.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. The Edge-Cloud data anomaly detection framework based on iterative optimization successfully improved the accuracy of anomaly detection (to 96.70%) while maintaining low latency in industrial sensor-cloud systems.
